text: The hyperscopic video captures the removal of a teratoma from the right ovary, showing the surgical instruments carefully excising the mass from the surrounding ovarian tissue.
The video shows the liver and gallbladder, with the anatomical structures clearly visible in the surgical field.

The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg position, tilting her head downwards to facilitate visualization of the pelvic region. The surgeon moves the omentum and intestines aside to clear the surgical field.

The laparoscopic view shows the uterus centrally positioned, with both ovaries visible on either side.
The laparoscopic view shows the right ovary with a visible teratoma, highlighting the anatomical structure and the abnormal growth.
It's not obvious at this time, but you will see later in the video.
The surgeon introduces additional laparoscopic trocars, carefully avoiding the visible inferior epigastric artery and vein, which are clearly seen in the laparoscopic view.
The surgeon introduces an additional laparoscopic trocar, ensuring it is positioned far from the visible inferior periosteic vessels to prevent injury during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to perform the same laparoscopic procedure on the left side, with visible anatomical structures including the uterus and both ovaries.
An additional laparoscopic trocar is inserted, avoiding the inferior epigastric artery and vein, which are visible in the surgical field.
The laparoscopic view shows the inferior periosteic vessels, as the surgeon carefully inserts a trocar away from these vessels to avoid injury.
The Da Vinci robotic system is docked, and the surgeon examines the ovary to locate the teratoma, using the robotic instruments for precise visualization.
The teratoma is visually occupying most of the ovary, appearing approximately five centimeters in size as indicated by the ultrasound image.
The surgeon uses endoshears to make a superficial incision on the ovary, carefully avoiding penetration into the capsule of the teratoma.

sometimes is possible to not enter it and sometimes depending on how thin that teratoma's capsule is, is sometimes impossible not to enter it.
Using the Da Vinci robotic system, the surgeon gently detaches the teratoma capsule from the ovarian capsule, carefully avoiding entry into the teratoma itself.
The surgeon observes the teratoma capsule, noting its very thin appearance, which may affect the ability to avoid entering the capsule during dissection.

The surgeon inadvertently enters the teratoma, revealing yellow, thick fluid. A suction irrigator is introduced to remove the fluid, aiming to reduce inflammation risk by extracting as much fluid as possible.
The surgeon carefully prevents the contents of the cyst, including hair, cartilage, or other tissues, from exiting the cyst during manipulation.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to repeatedly irrigate the pelvic cavity, effectively removing any residual teratoma content and fluid from the area.
The surgeon uses a Marilyn scissor to easily detach the teratoma capsule from the ovary capsule, demonstrating precise separation of the thin tissue layers.
After the cyst is completely removed, the surgeon uses a suction irrigator to clear the pelvis of any remaining yellowish liquid content, ensuring the surgical area is clean.
The surgeon irrigates the ovary, using gentle pressure with Maryland forceps to achieve hemostasis in areas of bleeding, ensuring minimal injury to the remaining ovarian capsule.
The surgeon performs chromopertubation by introducing dye into the uterus, visually confirming the dye's passage through the fallopian tubes.
The surgeon observes dye flowing through both fallopian tubes, confirming their patency and openness.
The surgeon visually confirms that the fallopian tubes are open, indicating successful chromoprotubation with dye visible exiting the tubes.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to clear the pelvic area, removing any remaining liquid content after the cyst removal.
The laparoscopic camera is repositioned to a different port, allowing the surgeon to introduce an endopoutra bag through the umbilical incision.
The surgeon places the cyst capsule of the teratoma into an endopoutra bag, preparing to remove it intact through the umbilical port.
